title: Quantum State Engineering Under Multiple Expectation-Value Constraints
abstract: This work introduces a formulation of quantum state engineering termed expectation-value targeting: the task of preparing a pure state whose expectation values with respect to a prescribed set of observables attain specified targets. This formulation subsumes standard ground-state preparation problems in quantum chemistry and many-body physics, while extending beyond variational energy minimization to multi-constraint state synthesis. The problem amounts to solving a system of nonlinear constraints on an exponentially large state space, for which no general efficient classical approaches are known. Variational quantum algorithms tackle this problem by restricting the search to a low-dimensional parameter space, and relying on classical optimization techniques for solutions. However, these approaches can become extremely ineffective for the present problem, where competing constraints can induce rugged landscapes and vanishing gradients (barren plateaus). Adaptive variational methods, in which the ansatz is constructed iteratively from a pool of candidate operators rather than fixed in advance, have been developed primarily for ground-state preparation. However, we show that the present problem also admits a similar construction. We introduce QUEST (Quantum Unitary Engineering of States to Target), a framework purpose-built for expectation-value targeting, in which the engineered state is constructed as a depth-adaptive sequence of Pauli rotations, with each rotation chosen to descend a sum-of-squared-residuals cost. QUEST provides a constructive route to expectation-value targeting, building the engineered state one Pauli rotation at a time, and establishes adaptive synthesis as a primitive for state preparation under multiple, potentially inconsistent target constraints.
summary: This paper presents QUEST, a new quantum algorithm for engineering pure states that satisfy multiple simultaneous expectation-value constraints. By using an adaptive, analytic approach to determine rotation angles, it avoids the vanishing gradient and local minima problems that plague standard variational quantum algorithms. The method is shown to be highly effective for preparing complex states, such as thermal states and Hubbard model configurations.
main_problem: The challenge of preparing a pure quantum state that simultaneously satisfies multiple prescribed expectation-value constraints, a task that generalizes standard ground-state preparation but suffers from vanishing gradients and local minima in standard variational algorithms.
main_result: The introduction of QUEST, a framework that uses an adaptive sequence of Pauli rotations with analytically determined angles to bypass the optimization failures (like barren plateaus and stalling) common in gradient-based variational methods.
method: An iterative, 'optimizer-free' algorithm that constructs a state via a depth-adaptive sequence of Pauli rotations, using either gradient-based selection or an exact analytic approach to determine rotation angles by reconstructing a trigonometric polynomial.
model_or_system: The framework is general and applicable to any n-qubit system, with specific demonstrations using the Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM) for pseudo-thermal state generation and the 1D Hubbard model for fermionic lattice simulations.
key_observables: A set of Hermitian operators (Pauli strings) and their target expectation values.
important_parameters: The number of qubits, the set of target values, the Pauli pool (e.g., weight-2 strings), and the weighting strategy for the cost function (e.g., spectral width or uncertainty-based).
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes the state is prepared via a Pauli path (a sequence of Pauli rotations) and that expectation values can be accessed via a quantum oracle.
figures_summary: Figures show the convergence of the RMS cost per constraint for different QUEST variants in noise-less and noisy scenarios, the evolution of expectation values during the algorithm, and the success of the method in capturing thermal and fermionic properties in the Hubbard model.
paper_structure: The paper formalizes the expectation-value targeting problem, critiques the failure modes of existing VQAs, introduces the QUEST framework and its various implementations (tG, bG, tE, bE), provides mathematical proofs for gradient vanishing, and demonstrates performance through TFIM and Hubbard model simulations.
why_interesting: It provides a robust alternative to ADAPT-VQE for many-body dynamics and state engineering, specifically offering a way to synthesize complex states (like thermal or fermionic states) without the instabilities of classical gradient-based optimization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Evolution of RMS cost per constraint for the four implementations of QUEST for the noise-less random Pauli string expectation value targeting of n = 10 qubit, with 100 constraints on the expectation values of random Pauli strings.
FIG. 2. Scatter plot of set targets vs prepared expectation values for the four implementations of QUEST, at the end of 30 iterations. The diagonal line y = x is shown for reference.

FIG. 3. Evolution of RMS cost per constraint for the four im- plementations of QUEST for the noisy random Pauli string expectation value targeting of n = 10 qubit, with 100 con- straints on the expectation values of random Pauli strings.

(26) and the right panel depicts the correlation Cψ(i, j) defined in Eq. (28), where |ψ⟩is the state synthesized by QUEST-bG at the terminal iteration.

FIG. 4. Scatter plot of set targets vs prepared expectation values for the four implementations of QUEST, computed in the terminal state of the four implementations. The diagonal line y = x is shown for reference. The grey vertical lines correspond to the ±σi bounds around the targets τi.
